Thank you. Morning. Um, I'm Sue Bid Rose. I'm the Chief Executive of AG Research, uh, and with me today, I have my chief scientist here from Palmerston, north Axel. Good morning. Uh, this is a significant moment in AG researcher's history. Today marks our final appearance before this committee as AG research. In fact, today marks our final day as AG research ahead of the imminent merger with our fellow Crown Research Institutes this afternoon, this evening, uh, to form the new public research organization before speaking about what lies ahead, I want to take a moment to acknowledge the end of an era and to reflect on the critical investment in science.

That success of governments represented by you here in this building have made over the last 30 years. The 45 million that we receive annually through the strategic science investment funds of funding is a cornerstone of AG researcher's success. While it represents around a quarter of our total annual revenue, it is a significant sum and one we believe has been money well spent.

On behalf of the AG Research Board, our scientists, technicians, and staff across New Zealand, I want to thank you for this enduring support. New Zealanders can be proud of what their investment has made possible. CIF has enabled us to deliver research that drives real world economic, environmental, and social returns.

It under underpins our support for the pastoral sector, which continue to play a crucial role in powering New Zealand's post COVID economic recovery. The impact of our science stretches far beyond the laboratory, shaping the strength of our primary industries, reinforcing our global reputation as a trusted food producer, and preparing us for the challenge of a changing climate and measuring return on that.

Investment in science isn't always as straightforward as the commercial world can be, but the benefits of breakthrough may take years to unfold. The value may merge across the supply chain or in the avoidance of costs, such as environmental damage, lost productivity, or reputational harm. But the evidence is clear when crown funded science is well targeted and delivered in partnership with industry and iwi.

It is transformative for New Zealand's economy are environment and our communities. Thanks to our science, new Zealand's become the first country in the world to successfully develop low methane emitting sheep genetic lines. And along with innovative methods for rapid screening, we've developed portable methane accumulation chambers for sheep and just at the moment for cattle that can be driven through the farm gate and used on farm to accurately measure emissions and select animals for breeding.

This is a world first achievement that is delivering actual, tangible on the ground results In reductions in methane, uh, we've got, um, New Zealand with a leader in biological control of agricultural pe. So for example, we introduced a small parasitic wasp as a biological control agent against CLO root weevil.

We used to make the total benefits of that program to be at least well, almost 500 million with benefits to accrue at a rate of about 150 million a year ongoing in a world. First we discovered epi glowy fungal end fights, which are now a vital component for perennial ryegrass in New Zealand. Uh, and the increase in efficiencies in milk and meat production have led to $3.6 billion estimated return in New Zealand economy.

This is a pretty startling piece of work having invested $12 million in the science 20, 30 years ago, and we are incredibly proud of it done with our partners, PGG Rights and Seeds, grasslands technology. Look, I have numerous other examples here, um, but in the, um, in the, uh, um, because of the time constraints.

So I'll, I'll come back to those when the time is appropriate. But I really want to finish by saying is this phase of our journey comes to an end? Can I reiterate our appreciation for the Crown's investment to and research through it's built capability, partnerships and science that makes a difference to productivity, to resilience, to environmental outcomes, and to New Zealand's reputation as a leader in sustainable agriculture?

Thank you for trusting us with that responsibility and for your stewardship of public investment in science. By sharpening our focus on commercialization, including greater support through grasslands technology and genomes nz global funding opportunities, we are ensuring our science delivers maximum impact and new revenue streams increasing our commercial as a proportion of our total revenue.

And at the same time, we've recently made tough but necessary decisions about reducing costs and to step back from underfunded research areas, our newly defined research priorities, targeting the major challenges in agriculture, agritech and AgriFood, where our science can actually make a difference, positions us strongly for the future. And together these moves put us in a sound strategic positions to successfully, uh, enter into today's merger.

And we are looking forward to that next chapter. Our scientists are genuinely excited about the changes ahead. We are pleased with the progress we've been making as ag research, the size and scale that this new research capability and capacity will involve and continuing our impact on our community.
